content.assessment: From a legal/governance perspective, this proposal seeks to insert a substantive procedural duty into a system that currently closes schools via ad hoc administrative acts ('temporary closure'/'suspension') without a dedicated statutory closure procedure (F-live-legal_and_governance-04). It would require new primary legislation or decree amendment to create an enforceable presumption, since existing accountability for reversing closures is informal and post-hoc rather than a codified appeal right (F-live-legal_and_governance-08). The 2026 restructuring plan offers a plausible legislative vehicle, but that plan is framed around restoring operational autonomy to directors and municipalities, not explicitly a closure-safeguard duty (F-live-legal_and_governance-09), so alignment is not guaranteed. A key governance risk is that any such duty would be administered by the same centralized maintainer (district centers under the Klebelsberg Center, now overseen by the Interior Ministry rather than an education ministry), creating a structural conflict of interest between the body deciding closures and the body assessing alternatives (F-live-legal_and_governance-01, F-live-legal_and_governance-02, F-live-legal_and_governance-03). Without an independent oversight or appeal body, the presumption risks being formally adopted but substantively hollow, echoing how village school directors already lack appointment or operational authority (F-live-legal_and_governance-02).
opportunities: Could be attached as an amendment within the planned 2026 structural overhaul, reducing the need for a wholly separate legislative process (F-live-legal_and_governance-09)
Formalizing the appeal/reversal process could reduce reliance on political protest as the only accountability lever (F-live-legal_and_governance-08)
strengths: Directly targets the documented legal gap: no dedicated statutory procedure exists for demographic-decline closures (F-live-legal_and_governance-04)
Could convert an existing informal, ad hoc reversal mechanism into a codified, predictable accountability channel (F-live-legal_and_governance-08)
The pending 2026 governance restructuring provides a concrete legislative opportunity to embed the safeguard (F-live-legal_and_governance-09)
threats: Central authorities may resist ceding discretion, given their track record of centralizing rather than devolving decision authority since 2013 (F-live-legal_and_governance-01, F-live-legal_and_governance-02)
Absent a genuine transfer of decision power, the safeguard risks becoming symbolic paperwork rather than a substantive check
weaknesses: Enforceability depends on genuine authority to halt closure, which is structurally difficult given the maintainer's own centralized control over the decision (F-live-legal_and_governance-01, F-live-legal_and_governance-02)
No independent oversight or appeal body currently exists in the governance structure to adjudicate alternatives assessments objectively (F-live-legal_and_governance-08)

Sectoral oversight now sits with the Interior Ministry rather than an education-focused authority, raising accountability questions about who would be responsible for enforcing an education-specific procedural duty (F-live-legal_and_governance-03)
